topic: Retrival-Augmented Generation for Legacy Code Refactoring

summaries_gpt.paper_id: Large Language Models for Software Engineering: A Systematic Literature Review
summaries_gpt.summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have significantly impacted numerous domains, including Software Engineering (SE). Many recent publications have explored LLMs applied to various SE tasks. Nevertheless, a comprehensive understanding of the application, effects, and possible limitations of LLMs on SE is still in its early stages. To bridge this gap, we conducted a Systematic Literature Review (SLR) on LLM4SE, with a particular focus on understanding how LLMs can be exploited to optimize processes and outcomes. We selected and analyzed 395 research articles from January 2017 to January 2024 to answer four key Research Questions (RQs). In RQ1, we categorize different LLMs that have been employed in SE tasks, characterizing their distinctive features and uses. In RQ2, we analyze the methods used in data collection, pre-processing, and application, highlighting the role of well-curated datasets for successful LLM for SE implementation. RQ3 investigates the strategies employed to optimize and evaluate the performance of LLMs in SE. Finally, RQ4 examines the specific SE tasks where LLMs have shown success to date, illustrating their practical contributions to the field. From the answers to these RQs, we discuss the current state-of-the-art and trends, identifying gaps in existing research, and highlighting promising areas for future study. Our artifacts are publicly available at https://github.com/security-pride/LLM4SE_SLR.

summaries_gpt.paper_id: Retrieval-augmented code completion for local projects using large language models
RAGdeterm: Deterministic retrieval-augmented generation for code generation
Software refactoring research with large language models: A systematic literature review
RepoAI: Automated code refactoring through multi-agent LLM orchestration and retrieval-augmented generation
Context-Aware Code Review Automation: A Retrieval-Augmented Approach
summaries_gpt.summary: We present a new method that views object detection as a direct set prediction problem. Our approach streamlines the detection pipeline, effectively removing the need for many hand-designed components like a non-maximum suppression procedure or anchor generation that explicitly encode our prior knowledge about the task. The main ingredients of the new framework, called DEtection TRansformer or DETR, are a set-based global loss that forces unique predictions via bipartite matching, and a transformer encoder-decoder architecture. Given a fixed small set of learned object queries, DETR reasons about the relations of the objects and the global image context to directly output the final set of predictions in parallel. The new model is conceptually simple and does not require a specialized library, unlike many other modern detectors. DETR demonstrates accuracy and run-time performance on par with the well-established and highly-optimized Faster RCNN baseline on the challenging COCO object detection dataset. Moreover, DETR can be easily generalized to produce panoptic segmentation in a unified manner. We show that it significantly outperforms competitive baselines.

summaries_gpt.paper_id: Code generation with large language models: A survey from neural program synthesis to autonomous software development.

final_summary_gpt: ## Summary Legacy software systems remain essential components of modern infrastructures but often accumulate architectural degradation, outdated programming practices, and insufficient documentation over extended maintenance periods. Such technical debt makes manual refactoring costly and error-prone, motivating extensive research into automated software transformation techniques. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have significantly expanded the capabilities of automated software engineering tools, including code understanding, generation, repair, and refactoring, although their performance remains constrained by limited contextual awareness of large code repositories [1]. Systematic analyses of LLM-based software engineering have shown that these models can effectively assist developers in programming-related tasks but still exhibit limitations such as hallucination, inconsistent adherence to project conventions, and difficulties in reasoning over repository-level dependencies [1]. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a promising paradigm for addressing these limitations by enriching LLM prompts with external information retrieved from source repositories, documentation, or structured representations of software artifacts [2]. Recent empirical studies in code generation have demonstrated that retrieval mechanisms can improve the quality of generated code by providing relevant local project context, with retrieval-augmented approaches showing measurable improvements over non-retrieval baselines in code completion tasks [2]. In software engineering settings, retrieval strategies that preserve structural information, such as dependencies and object-oriented relationships, have been proposed to improve the consistency and reproducibility of generated code modifications [3].  The integration of RAG with automated refactoring is particularly relevant for legacy systems, where successful transformations require understanding both localized implementation details and broader architectural constraints. Contemporary reviews of LLM-assisted refactoring indicate that these models show substantial potential for automating refactoring activities, but reliability, validation, and contextual understanding remain major challenges [4]. Repository-level RAG frameworks have consequently incorporated multi-agent workflows, specialized retrieval mechanisms, and independent validation strategies to achieve more reliable large-scale code modifications [5]. Experimental evidence from recent studies suggests that such architectures can improve repository-level refactoring performance when compared with direct generation approaches, although errors related to code generation and semantic preservation remain unresolved challenges [5]. Additional research has demonstrated the value of context-aware retrieval for related software engineering activities, including code review automation and repository-aware code generation, suggesting that richer contextual information can enhance the relevance and accuracy of model outputs [6,7]. Therefore, Retrieval-Augmented Generation represents a promising direction for legacy code refactoring because it combines the generative abilities of LLMs with project-specific knowledge retrieval; however, further research is required to improve scalability, guarantee behavioral preservation, and establish rigorous evaluation methodologies for industrial adoption [1,4].
